coredata.dc:description: Developing economically efficient adsorption-photocatalysis bifunctional uranium-containing wastewater treatment agents is significant for environmental remediation and governance. In this study, a novel adsorptive photocatalyst based on low-cost hybrid GQDs/Sn3O4 nanocomposite (GSO) was fabricated using a one-step hydrothermal method for highly efficient synergistic uranium removal. The batch adsorption experiments revealed that the adsorption process of GSO-15 on U(VI) matched the pseudo-second-order model and Langmuir isotherm, with a maximum Langmuir adsorption capacity (qmax) of 226.51 mg·g−1 at pH 6.0. In addition, U(VI) reduction by GSO-15 under visible light irradiation demonstrated excellent photocatalytic performance, while the optimal removal rate of U(VI) by synergistic adsorption-photoreduction was up to 99.67 %, which was significantly higher than that of physicochemical adsorption. Furthermore, the mechanism of GSO-15 photoreduction of U(VI) was explored through various methods, indicating that the photocatalytic performance was primarily attributed to its strong visible light absorbability, narrow bandgap, and efficient separation and transfer of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. After five cycles of photocatalytic reduction desorption, the removal rate remained above 95 %, indicating excellent structural stability and considerable recyclability. The decrease in the removal rate was negligible in the presence of coexisting metal ions. This study provides novel insights into the design and fabrication of efficient adsorptive photocatalytic materials for treating radioactive uranium-containing wastewater.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of novel adsorptive photocatalyst GQDs/Sn3O4 for highly efficient synergistic removal of U(Ⅵ)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing economically efficient adsorption-photocatalysis bifunctional uranium-containing wastewater treatment agents is significant for environmental remediation and governance. In this study, a novel adsorptive photocatalyst based on low-cost hybrid GQDs/Sn3O4 nanocomposite (GSO) was fabricated using a one-step hydrothermal method for highly efficient synergistic uranium removal. The batch adsorption experiments revealed that the adsorption process of GSO-15 on U(VI) matched the pseudo-second-order model and Langmuir isotherm, with a maximum Langmuir adsorption capacity (qmax) of 226.51 mg·g−1 at pH 6.0. In addition, U(VI) reduction by GSO-15 under visible light irradiation demonstrated excellent photocatalytic performance, while the optimal removal rate of U(VI) by synergistic adsorption-photoreduction was up to 99.67 %, which was significantly higher than that of physicochemical adsorption. Furthermore, the mechanism of GSO-15 photoreduction of U(VI) was explored through various methods, indicating that the photocatalytic performance was primarily attributed to its strong visible light absorbability, narrow bandgap, and efficient separation and transfer of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. After five cycles of photocatalytic reduction desorption, the removal rate remained above 95 %, indicating excellent structural stability and considerable recyclability. The decrease in the removal rate was negligible in the presence of coexisting metal ions. This study provides novel insights into the design and fabrication of efficient adsorptive photocatalytic materials for treating radioactive uranium-containing wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The author had shared the link to the data at the Attach File step.

ce:section.ce:para: Nuclear energy, as a green and clean energy source, is characterized by low carbon emissions and remarkable energy density, positioning it as one of the most promising future energy sources[1]. Uranium serves as a pivotal nuclear fuel and a heavy metal element with chemical, radioactive, and biological toxicities[2,3]. With the rapid development of the global atomic energy industry, widespread application of nuclear fuel cycles and nuclear technology, a large amount of uranium-containing wastewater will inevitably be generated, posing a serious threat to ecological environment, human health, and sustainable social development[4–6]. Therefore, the efficient removal of U(VI) from uranium-containing wastewater is an imperative issue with important environmental and social significance.

To date, researchers have developed various methods for removing U(VI) from uranium-containing wastewater, such as adsorption[7], membrane separation[8], ion exchange[9], bioremediation[10], and electrochemical methods[11]. Among them, adsorption has been widely researched due to its high efficiency, simple operational design, and low cost. Currently, there is an increasing number of advanced materials with excellent adsorption capacity, high selectivity, and environmental-friendly performance, including inorganic nanomaterials[12], biological materials[13], polymer materials[14], porous materials[15], etc[16–18]. Jin et al.[19] reported that PVA-Urea-PA porous polymer film presented excellent uranium adsorption capacity, up to 259.1 mg·g−1. Huang et al.[20] synthesized a phytic acid-modified chitosan material via an electrodeposition process and achieved a high uranium removal capacity of 429.1 mg·g−1. However, the practical application of adsorption methods for treating uranium-containing wastewater is still restricted to varying degrees by issues such as poor adsorbent selectivity and slow adsorption rates.

Photocatalytic technology offers an efficient solution for the issues mentioned above and remediating uranium-containing wastewater[21–24]. Photocatalysis can reduce highly mobile U(VI) to insoluble U(IV), thereby treating U(VI) contamination. This method boasts advantages such as a green economy, high efficiency, low cost, and performance stability, presenting great potential for development in the environmental energy sector. With the advancement of photocatalytic technology, numerous photocatalytic materials have emerged for U(VI) reduction, including g-C3N4 [25], TiO2 [26], CdS[27], SnS2 [28], etc. Li et al.[29] reported the photocatalytic reduction of U(VI) using TiO2, achieving a high uranium extraction of up to 3960 mg·g−1 under unsaturated conditions. Liu et al.[28] employed in-situ synthesized SnS2/COF and achieved a high uranium extraction capacity of 1123.3 mg·g−1 in rare earth mine tailings wastewater. Despite the remarkable performance of certain materials in the photocatalytic treatment of uranium-containing wastewater, developing new photocatalytic materials with high catalytic activity, high recovery efficiency, and simple operation remains a significant challenge.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) represent a distinctive zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial composed mainly of sp3-hybridized amorphous carbon and sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon nanocrystals, with an internal graphene structure providing GQDs with a large specific surface area and high accessibility of active sites[30–33]. Additionally, GQDs possess continuously tunable bandgap widths, luminescent characteristics, good photostability, outstanding water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, ease of modification, and preparation, which have found extensive applications in various fields such as bioimaging, fluorescence sensing, and photocatalysis[34–36]. They are regarded as a rising star among carbon nanomaterials. Bhuvaneswari et al.[37] fabricated a novel electrochemical non-enzymatic chemical sensor based on hybrid GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC nanocomposite for creatinine (CTN) detection, demonstrating good repeatability, reproducibility, and sensitivity. Ding et al.[38] reported a supramolecular hydrogel (GQDs-gsh) that can effectively detect and remove U(VI) based on its unique pH-responsive characteristics, while also enabling GQDs recovery.

Sn3O4 is a typical n-type semiconductor, where the mixed valence of Sn2+ and Sn4+ forms an ideal band structure, exhibiting excellent light absorption compared to other single tin oxide states. It possesses rich oxygen vacancies, giving it outstanding physicochemical properties[39–41]. Sn3O4 is a novel environmentally friendly semiconductor with abundant reserves, showing high application potential and research value in photocatalysis, photoelectric conversion, and energy storage[39,42]. However, its relatively high oxidation potential results in lower activity in photocatalytic reduction applications, and the construction of heterojunction structures is an effective strategy for augmenting its photocatalytic activity[43]. Various Sn3O4-based semiconductor materials, such as Co3O4/Sn3O4 [44], Sn3O4/g-C3N4 [45], N-CQDs/Sn3O4 [46], TiO2 NTs/Sn3O4 [47], have been successfully synthesized and utilized in the field of photocatalysis, demonstrating excellent performance in pollutant degradation. However, there is limited research on the construction of Sn3O4-based heterojunctions for photocatalytic reduction of U(VI). It is worth noting that combining GQDs with Sn3O4 to build high-performance heterojunctions for remediating uranium-containing wastewater shows great promise.

Alternatively, hydrothermal method is the primary approach for selectively preparing nanocatalytic materials with different morphologies and crystal structures by modulating precursor, reaction time, and temperature conditions[48]. Compared to other preparation methods, such as the coprecipitation method, which can cause significant agglomeration, poor crystallinity, and uneven particle size distribution[49], and mechanochemical method which has the disadvantages of high energy consumption and the potential for introducing impurities during process[50]. Researchers increasingly favor hydrothermal method due to the advantages of economic and environmentally friendly nature, ease of operation, small particle size, high purity, and controllable morphology[51]. Herein, a novel adsorptive photocatalyst GQDs/Sn3O4 (GSO) nanocomposite material was constructed using a one-step hydrothermal method with varying ratios of GQDs and Sn3O4. The microstructures and morphologies of the materials were investigated using various characterization techniques, and the optical and photoelectrical properties were analyzed. The adsorption properties of the GSO nanocomposite material in the dark and synergistic adsorption and photoreduction performance under visible light illumination were explored. Furthermore, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of U(VI) by GSO was investigated, along with its selectivity, recyclability, and structural stability, furnishing an effective strategy for tackling real environmental issues in the future.

Tin(II) chloride dihydrate and Trisodium citrate dihydrate were procured from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd; graphene quantum dots were obtained from Guangzhou Shunzhuo Energy Technology Co., Ltd; sodium hydroxide, acetone, phenolphthalein, concentrated ammonia solution, concentrated hydrochloric acid, and uranyl trioxide were all sourced from the National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals utilized in this study were of analytical grade and used without any subsequent purification.

1.128 g of SnCl2·2 H2O was dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water, 3.676 g of Na3C6H5O7·2 H2O was added during stirring, mixed uniformly, and after stirring and ultrasonication for 0.5 h, the pH of the above solution was fine-tuned to 6.0 using a 1.0 mol·L−1 NaOH solution. After continued stirring and sonication for 0.5 h, a GQDs solution of various mass fractions was added, followed by stirring and ultrasonication for 0.5 h. The mixture was subsequently transferred to a stainless steel high-pressure reaction vessel, lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. Following a reaction duration of 453 K for 12 h, the mixture was gradually cooled to room temperature, the resulting precipitate was then subjected to centrifugation and washed thrice with both deionized water and anhydrous ethanol. Finally, it was dried thoroughly in an oven at 333 K for 12 h to obtain a yellow-brown powder of GQDs/Sn3O4 with different GQDs mass fractions (5 %, 10 %, 15 %, 20 %), denoted as GSO-5, GSO-10, GSO-15, GSO-20. In the absence of GQDs, Sn3O4 was also prepared using the same hydrothermal method. The preparation process of GQDs/Sn3O4 is shown in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Batch adsorption experiments in dark environment
ce:section.ce:para:
The impact of different ratios, pH values, contact times, dosages, and temperatures on the adsorption performance of the material towards U(VI) in the dark environment was studied through batch static adsorption experiments. 5 mg of prepared material was homogeneously dispersed in a 25 mL U(VI) solution, and a negligible volume of 0.1 mol·L−1 HCl or NaOH solution was added to fine-tune the pH of the U(VI) solution before placing it in a thermostatic shaker. After a certain period, 2 mL of the supernatant was taken, filtered through a 0.22-μm syringe filter, and subsequently analyzed for uranium concentration. The uranium concentration was determined by the Azon coupling III spectrophotometric method and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). The removal rate and removal amount of U(VI) by the samples were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2).

where C0 and Ce (mg·L−1) are the initial and instantaneous residual concentration of the sample at time t, respectively. m (g) is the dosage of the sample, and V (L) is the volume of the reaction solution. R (%) and qe (mg·g−1) are the removal rate and removal amount of U (VI) by the sample, respectively.

The photocatalytic experiments were conducted at 298 K. The pH of the photocatalytic experiments was also adjusted using 0.1 mol·L−1 HCl or NaOH solution, which corresponds to the optimal pH for adsorption experiments in a dark environment. Typically, a 500 W Xenon lamp (equipped with a 420 nm cutoff filter) served as the illumination source, and the photocatalyst and 1 mL of methanol were added to 25 mL of U(VI) solution with a given pH and concentration. The uranium concentration was ascertained by the same method as in the adsorption experiments. In addition, the influence of contact time, U(VI) solution concentration, different light sources and coexisting ions on the photocatalytic separation of U(VI) on the catalyst was investigated. Cycling stability tests were performed to assess the regeneration performance of the photocatalysts and the uranium on the catalyst surface was eluted with 0.1 M HCl after each cycle.

The crystal structure and parameters of the materials were analyzed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD). The microscopic morphology and structural composition of the materials were obtained using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). The functional group composition was analyzed by Fourier-transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The compositional composition of the materials was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The surface area of Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (SBET) and the pore structure of the materials were also analyzed. The optical and optoelectronic properties of the materials were analyzed by UV–vis DRS spectrum, Mott-Schottky curve, transient photocurrent response, EIS Nyquist plot and photoluminescence spectra (PL). were used to analyze the optical and optoelectronic properties of the materials, and the active free radicals were determined using electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR). Specific instrumental parameters can be found in the supplementary materials.

The adsorption kinetics process was investigated using pseudo-first-order (PFO) and pseudo-second-order (PSO) models. Adsorption isotherm analysis was conducted using Langmuir, Freundlich isotherm models and thermodynamics. The photocatalytic reaction kinetics was analyzed using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood equation. The aforementioned calculation formulas are detailed in the supplementary materials.

The morphology and microstructure of GQDs/Sn3O4 were studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). According to Fig. 2(a) and (b), GQDs/Sn3O4 presents a unique nanoflower structure, and due to the small particle size of GQDs, abundant active sites are exposed, which promotes its tight assembly[52], forming a three-dimensional nanoflower structure with augmented specific surface area and excellent stability. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) images are shown in Fig. 2(c)-(e). As discerned from Fig. 2(c), the C element is mainly distributed on the surface and between the layers of the nanosphere structure, and the carbon skeleton of GQDs provides support and connection for Sn3O4 nanoparticles, while Sn3O4 is embedded between GQDs, forming a tight composite structure. Fig. 2(d) shows that GQDs/Sn3O4 contains only three elements, C, O and Sn, no other impurity peaks are detected, and the strength of the peaks is proportional to the content of elements in the sample, which is basically consistent with the theoretical value. According to Fig. 2(e), C, O, and Sn elements are uniformly distributed in the GQDs/Sn3O4 composite.

The physical structure of the material was analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD). As evident in Fig. 3(a), for pure Sn3O4 sample, obvious characteristic peaks appearing at 27.080°, 31.727°, 32.315°, 33.014°, 37.072° and 51.688° are typical Sn3O4 crystal surfaces (111), (−210), (−121), (210), (130) and (−1−32), consistent with the standard card (JCPDS NO.16–0737) peak position. The results indicate that high-purity Sn3O4 has been successfully synthesized. In contrast, no additional diffraction peaks appear in the composite due to the trace amount of GQDs, suggesting that the introduction of GQDs has no effect on the crystallinity of Sn3O4.

In addition, the presence of GQDs in the composite material was detected by Fourier-transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), as shown in Fig. 3(b). The FT-IR spectrum of Sn3O4 exhibits a peak at 487.7 cm−1 from the Sn-O tensile vibration[42]. The addition of GQDs has no substantial effect on the FT-IR spectra of Sn3O4, which may be due to the small weight load of GQDs. The FT-IR results of GQDs/Sn3O4 could match well with that of pure Sn3O4, proving that the composites were successfully synthesized. The new characteristic peaks at 1230 cm−1, 1630 cm−1 and 2360 cm−1 are attributed to the tensile vibrations of C−O, CC and CO, respectively, indicating the presence of GQDs in the composite[53,54]. The −OH tensile vibration at 3429 cm−1 and −OH bending vibration band at 1622 cm−1 of GQDs/Sn3O4 nanocomposite become narrow, which means that the distribution of water molecules adsorbed onto the surface is relatively uniform[53,55]. At the same time, the band representing the Sn-O tensile vibration at 592 cm−1 widens and shifts slightly towards higher frequencies. This finding signifies a substantial interaction between GQDs and Sn3O4 [55].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to further characterize the chemical state at the interface of the materials. Fig. 4(a) shows the full spectrum of GSO-15, confirming the existence of Sn, O, and C element in GSO-15. For high-resolution Sn 3d spectra (Fig. 4(b)), two characteristic peaks of Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2 are observed. The peaks representing Sn 3d5/2 are decomposed into 486.8 eV and 486.2 eV, which can be identified as Sn4+ and Sn2+, while the peaks representing Sn 3d3/2 are decomposed into 495.2 eV and 494.6 eV, which can be identified as Sn4+ and Sn2+ [56]. In the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4(c)), the XPS peaks at binding energies of 530.7 eV, 531.9 eV and 532.9 eV may be attributed to O−Sn2+, O−Sn4+ and CO, respectively[45,46]. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4(d)) can be typically decomposed into three peaks of 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV and 288.8 eV, which belong to the, CC/C−C, C−O and CO groups, respectively, and are mainly generated by GQDs[57]. The above analysis further confirms the successful synthesis of GQDs/Sn3O4 nanocomposite.

In addition, the specific surface area Bruner-Emmett-Teller (SBET) and pore structure of the sample were also assessed. In Fig. 3(c), the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of GSO-15 is a type IV isotherm accompanied by a type H3 hysteretic loop. Combined with the pore size distribution, it can be seen that GSO-15 is a mesoporous structure with a pore size lower than 20 nm, which is attributed to the stacking of nanosheets[53,58]. It is worth noting that Table S1 shows the SBET of GSO-15 is 47.02 m2·g−1 and the average pore size is 14.70 nm. Compared with other composites of the same type in Table S1, GSO-15 has higher SBET and mesoppore characteristics, representing the availability of more reaction sites and conducive to U(VI) transmission and diffusion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: U(VI) adsorption ability in dark environment
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of U(VI) on the photocatalyst will have a significant impact on the subsequent photocatalytic process[59]. The U(VI) separation performance of GQDs/Sn3O4 nanocomposite was studied by batch adsorption experiments in dark environment. The mass ratio of GQDs/Sn3O4 in composite materials is a pivotal factor influencing the adsorption of U(VI). Fig. 5(a) clearly illustrates that the mass ratio of GQDs in GQDs/Sn3O4 increases the removal efficiency of U(VI), and the removal rate of the composite is significantly higher than that of Sn3O4, which may be because the high specific surface area of GQDs/Sn3O4 provided by the three-dimensional nanosphere structure can effectively increase the adsorption active site of U(VI)[60]. When the mass ratio of GQDs/Sn3O4 is 15 %, the removal rate of U(VI) reaches 88.65 %. However, the removal efficiency decreased with the increase of GQDs, which may be because excessive GQDs covered the active site on Sn3O4 and inhibited adsorption.

pH is a crucial parameter that significantly impacts the surface charge of the adsorbent and the chemical morphology of uranyl ions in the solution[61]. As depicted in Fig. 5(b), when pH value rises from 3 to 9, the adsorption efficiency of GSO-15 nanocomposite for U(VI) exhibits a trend of initial enhancement followed by a decrease. Notably, the optimal pH for achieving the maximum U(VI) separation rate is at pH=6.0. Fig. S1 shows the morphological distribution curve of U(VI) at different pH values (3−9)[4]. When pH<3, uranium in the solution mainly exists in the form of
UO
2
2
+
ion[62], and a substantial quantity of H+ in the solution competes with
UO
2
2
+
for the adsorption site on the surface of GSO-15, while the positive charge repulsion between uranium ion and GSO-15 leads to a low uranium removal rate[38]. With the increase of pH, uranyl hydroxide ions such as
UO
2
OH
+
and
UO
2
3
OH
5
+
were gradually formed in the solution, providing more binding sites for the reaction[4]. However, when pH>6, uranyl ions begin to exist in the form of anions and electrostatic repulsion occurs with the surface of GSO-15, which is not conducive to adsorption.

Fig. 5(c) elucidates the influence of GSO-15 dosage on the adsorption of U(VI). The efficiency of U(VI) adsorption by GSO-15 augmented with the escalation of its dosage, which was attributed to the augmentation of adsorption sites on the GSO-15 surface with an increase in dosage. However, as the dosage further increased, the adsorption rate gradually stabilized, presumably due to the agglomeration of GSO-15 resulting from its excessive quantity, thereby diminishing the effective area and adsorption sites per unit mass of GSO-15. To realize the concept of saving consumables while efficiently adsorbing U(VI), the dosage of GSO-15 was 0.20 g·L−1 for the subsequent experiments.

The in-depth analysis of adsorption kinetics and thermodynamics were investigated to elucidate the adsorption mechanism of GQDs/Sn3O4 nanocomposite for uranium. As depicted in Fig. 5(d), (e) and Table S2, the experimental data were fitted using pseudo-first-order (PFO, Eq. S1) and pseudo-second-order (PSO, Eq. S2) models. For all GQDs/Sn3O4 nanocomposite, the fitting correlation coefficients (R2 ≥ 0.9861) of the PSO model were higher than those of the PFO model (R2 ≥ 0.7229), and the calculated adsorption capacity was closer to the experimental data. This indicates that the PSO model better fits the adsorption kinetics of GQDs/Sn3O4 for uranium, further proving that the adsorption process is primarily chemical adsorption[63].

The relationship between the initial concentration and the equilibrium state of the sample at a certain temperature was further investigated, and the experimental data were fitted and analyzed using the Langmuir (Eq. S3) and Freundlich (Eq. S4) isothermal models. As depicted in Fig. 5(f), the adsorption capacity significantly increased and then gradually reached equilibrium with the increase in the initial U(VI) concentration at 288 K, 298 K, and 308 K. As shown in Fig. 5(g), (h), and Table S4, the correlation coefficient (R2 ≥ 0.9908) of the Langmuir model was higher than that of the Freundlich model (R2 ≥ 0.9315), and the adsorption capacity was closer to the experimental values (226.51 mg·g−1, 209.64 mg·g−1, 203.80 mg·g−1), indicating that the GSO-15 surface has uniform adsorption energy for U(VI). Consequently, the adsorption process can be better described by the surface monolayer adsorption mechanism[64]. Fig. 5(i) presents the Gibbs free energy (ΔG0), entropy (ΔS0), and enthalpy (ΔH0) obtained through fitting and calculation of the thermodynamic equation (Eq. S5, S6), and the relevant parameters are enumerated in Table S5. The positive ΔH0 value and negative ΔG0 value suggest that the adsorption of U(VI) by GSO-15 is a spontaneous endothermic process[65]. Meanwhile, the gradual decrease in ΔG0 value with increasing temperature indicates that higher temperatures are more favorable for the adsorption of U(VI) by GSO-15[17]. The positive ΔS0 value indicates an increase in the degree of disorder at the solid-liquid interface during the adsorption process.

The FT-IR spectra of GSO-15 before and after uranium loading is shown in
Fig. 7(d). A new characteristic peak corresponding to OU=O appeared at 917 cm−1 after uranium adsorption of GSO-15[66], while the intensities of C-O and CC were slightly weakened, and the peak of the −OH vibration was shifted from 3429 cm−1 shifted to 3415 cm−1, indicating the involvement of uranium adsorption reaction. The chemical state of GSO-15 before and after adsorption was analyzed by XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 7(c), the characteristic U 4 f peak appeared after the adsorption of uranium, indicating that uranium was successfully adsorbed. The high-resolution spectrum of Sn 3d is shown in Fig. 4(b), the peaks previously attributed to Sn4+ and Sn2+ in Sn 3d3/2, and Sn4+ and Sn2+ in Sn 3d5/2 were shifted to 495.5 eV, 494.7 eV, 487.1 eV, and 486.1 eV after adsorption, respectively, which may be due to the ligand reaction with uranium[66]. The high-resolution O 1 s spectra are shown in Fig. 4(c), where each functional group moved towards higher binding energies after uranium adsorption, suggesting that the oxygen-containing functional groups in GSO-15 were coordinated with uranium, then leading to a decrease in the electron cloud density[67]. For the high-resolution C 1 s spectra in Fig. 4(d), a decrease in the peak areas of C-C and CO after adsorption was observed, suggesting possible involvement in surface complexation reactions[17].

The photocatalytic activity of GQDs/Sn3O4 nanocomposite on U(VI) was evaluated through photocatalytic experiments, as shown in Fig. 6. When the illumination begins, the concentration of U(VI) in the solution decreases rapidly. With the extension of irradiation time, the rate of concentration reduction becomes slower until it reaches a plateau at 30 min (Fig. 6(a)). Evidently, the equilibrium time of the simultaneous adsorption and photoreduction process is significantly reduced when compared to pure physical chemical adsorption, and the removal capacity is improved considerably. In particular, GSO-15 nanocomposite showed that more than 99.67 % of U(VI) was removed after 30 min of visible light irradiation, indicating that the catalyst could accelerate the photocatalytic separation process of U(VI) by increasing the loading of uranium ions on the photocatalyst surface[68]. Langmuir-Hinshelwood equation (Eq.S7) was used to analyze the photocatalytic reaction kinetics. As can be seen from Table S3, the rate constant (0.1226 min−1) of the GSO-15 nanocomposite is higher than that of other samples, which is attributed to its unique nanosphere structure providing abundant active sites for photocatalytic reduction of U(VI). Compared with reported photocatalysts ( Table 1), GSO-15 nanocomposite shows obvious advantages. In addition, the U(VI) removal ability of GSO-15 was studied under different initial U(VI) concentrations of dark and light sources. As depicted in Fig. 6(c), GSO-15 nanocomposite exhibits near-complete removal of U(VI) from a 5 mg·L−1 uranium solution under both dark and light conditions. Upon elevating the initial U(VI) concentration to 50 and 100 mg·L−1, the uranium removal rates in the dark environment were 68.74 % and 41.86 %, respectively (Fig. 6(d), (e)). In stark contrast, GSO-15 achieved a remarkably higher U(VI) removal efficiency of 90.48 % and 71.45 % under visible light irradiation, significantly surpassing the performance of pure physicochemical adsorption. The above indicates that the photoreduction reaction can significantly improve the U(VI) removal performance of GQDs/Sn3O4.

Given the potential advantages of practical applications, solar-driven photocatalysis has become an important direction for catalyst development. The photocatalytic separation of U(VI) on GSO-15 nanocomposite under sunlight was studied (Fig. 6(f)). Control experiments were set up in a dark environment, and two other groups were irradiated using distinct light sources. Both light sources displayed superior U(VI) removal efficiency when compared to the dark environment, and the photoresponse characteristics under sunlight irradiation were very similar to those under 500 W Xenon lamp irradiation, indicating that GSO-15 nanocomposite is promising for U(VI) separation under sunlight.

In order to investigate the selectivity of GSO-15 nanocomposite towards U(VI), the effects of various coexisting metal ions on the photoreduction of U(VI) by GSO-15 were studied, as shown in Fig. 7(a). Despite the concentration of coexisting metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Co2+, Mn2+, and Al3+) reaching 10 times that of U(VI), they had only a slight impact on the removal rate of U(VI). This result demonstrates the excellent selective removal ability of GSO-15 towards U(VI).

Recyclability is a pivotal factor in assessing the economic performance of materials. As depicted in Fig. 7(b), after five cycles of photoreduction and elution, the photocatalytic separation efficiency of GSO-15 towards U(VI) remains at 96.65 %, representing a mere 3.02 % decrease from its initial U(VI) removal rate (99.67 %). The high stability of GSO-15 is evidenced by XPS and FT-IR characterizations of uranium-loaded GSO-15. As shown in Fig. 7(c), the XPS spectrum of uranium-loaded GSO-15 exhibits no significant changes compared to fresh GSO-15, apart from the prominent U 4 f characteristic peak, indicating that GSO-15 has good surface structure and chemical state stability. Fig. 7(d) presents the FT-IR spectra of GSO-15 and uranium-loaded GSO-15, which exhibits no significant changes except for the characteristic uranium (OU=O) peak at 917 cm−1, demonstrating the good stability of the functional groups on GSO-15. The above results suggest that GSO-15 possesses remarkable recyclability.

The separation and transfer of the carrier is a crucial factor in the photocatalytic reduction of U(VI). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) was employed to determine the optical properties of the photocatalysts. As depicted in Fig. 8(a), the introduction of GQDs into Sn3O4 results in an increased absorption edge. This is ascribed to the two-photon or multi-photon reactions in GQDs, enabling excitation at shorter wavelengths and thereby enhancing visible light adsorption[57]. The band gap energy (Eg) of the obtained samples was determined using the Tauc method, as shown in Fig. 8(b). It can be concluded that GSO-15 (2.52 eV) possesses a lower Eg than pure Sn3O4 (2.63 eV), potentially enhancing the photoreduction rate and making better use of visible light. The Mott–Schottky curves of the samples are presented in Fig. 8(c). The flat band potentials (EFB) of GSO-15 and Sn3O4 are −0.71 eV and −0.51 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl), corresponding to −0.51 eV and −0.31 eV (vs. Normal Hydrogen Electrode, NHE). Furthermore, the conduction band potential (ECB) of n-type semiconductors is typically 0.1–0.3 eV smaller than the EFB [53], and the EFB and ECB are distinguished by a voltage of 0.2 eV. Therefore, the final ECB values of GSO-15 and Sn3O4 are −0.71 eV (vs. NHE) and −0.51 eV (vs. NHE), indicating that GSO-15 has a more negative conduction band potential, resulting in a stronger ability to reduce O2 to ⋅O2 −. Subsequently, based on the equation Eg = EVB − ECB, the valence band potentials (EVB) of GSO-15 and Sn3O4 were determined to be 1.81 eV and 2.12 eV, respectively. Furthermore, we have determined the energy band structure diagram of GSO-15 and Sn3O4, as depicted in Fig. 9(a). Since the ECB of GSO-15 is lower than the reduction potentials of
UO
2
2
+
/UO2 (0.411 eV) and
UO
2
2
+
/U4+ (0.267 eV), the photoreduction of U(VI) to U(IV) using GSO-15 is theoretically feasible[68]. Based on these conclusions, we attribute the excellent photocatalytic performance of GSO-15 to its strong visible light absorbability and narrow bandgap.

Generally speaking, the separation and effective migration of electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs generated during the photocatalytic process are crucial. Therefore, a comprehensive assessment of the photovoltaic properties was conducted utilizing transient photocurrent (I-t), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and steady-state photoluminescence (PL). As shown in Fig. 8(d), the photocurrent density of GSO-15 is significantly higher than that of pure Sn3O4, indicating that the composite material can effectively separate e−-h+ pairs. The EIS plot in Fig. 8(e) shows that GSO-15 has a smaller arc radius, indicating a relatively small charge transfer resistance, which can enhance the separation of electrons and holes and facilitate rapid transport. Fig. 8(f) depicts the PL spectrum, where the intensity of the GSO-15 peak is lower than that of pure Sn3O4, indicating a low rate of photogenerated e−-h+ pair recombination in the GSO-15 composite. This result is well aligned with the photocurrent findings. In conclusion, the photoelectric performance evaluation demonstrates that the formed GSO-15 composite significantly enhances carrier separation, leading to superior photocatalytic performance.

Fig. 7(c) presents the XPS spectra of GSO-15 after the adsorption of U(VI) and the photocatalytic reduction of U(VI). The presence of O 1 s, Sn 3d, C 1 s, and U 4 f elements in GSO-15 is observed, and the emergence of a new U 4 f peak after adsorption and photoreduction confirms the successful adsorption and reduction of U(VI). Fig. 9(d) shows the high-resolution U 4 f spectrum of GSO-15 after photocatalytic reduction. The XPS spectra of U 4 f7/2 (382.4 eV) and U 4 f5/2 (393.1 eV) are attributed to U(VI)[76], while the XPS spectra of U 4 f7/2 (380.6 eV) and U 4 f5/2 (391.4 eV) are attributed to U(IV)[77]. This result verifies the successful photocatalytic reduction of U(VI) to U(IV) under visible light irradiation. It is noteworthy that, albeit both adsorption and photoreduction can eliminate U(VI) from the solution, their underlying removal mechanisms are intrinsically disparate. Typically, the photoreduction of U(VI) involves a chemical process that is concomitantly accompanied by the formation of reactive radicals. Nonetheless, adsorption represents a prevalent physical phenomenon that is devoid of producing radicals or intermediate compounds. In the aforementioned research, the adsorption mechanism was elucidated through kinetic and thermodynamic models and XPS analysis, etc. In that case, the detection of radicals can provide a more effective explanation of the photocatalytic reduction process.

The active species during the photoreduction of U(VI) were investigated through radical capture experiments. CH3OH, K2(SO4)2, p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), and tert-butanol (TBA) were used as inhibitors for h+, e−, •O2 −, and •OH, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2, the addition of methanol significantly enhances the removal rate of U(VI), which is attributed to the capture of h+ by methanol, thereby improving the separation efficiency of e− and h+. The inclusion of TBA has a negligible effect on the removal of U(VI), indicating that the role of •OH in the photocatalytic process is negligible. Conversely, the addition of p-BQ and K2(SO4)2 significantly reduces the removal rate of U(VI), suggesting that •O2 − and e− are the primary reactive species in the photocatalytic process. EPR was employed to determine the active species, namely •O2 − and •OH radicals. As evident from Fig. 9(b) and (c), strong characteristic signals of DMPO-•O2 − and DMPO-•OH radical adducts were detected under light irradiation, whereas no signals were detected in the dark, indicating the presence of •O2 − and •OH radicals in the photocatalytic system. Based on the above results and analysis, the possible catalytic reduction mechanism of GSO-15 for U(VI) under visible light can be summarized in Eqs. (3)-(8) and Fig. 10. Under visible light irradiation, GSO-15 is excited to generate e− and h+ (Eq. (3)). The e− can react with O2 to produce •O2 − (Eq. (4)), and the generated •O2 − and e− can convert soluble
UO
2
2
+
into UO2 or U4+ (Eqs. (5) and (6)). Meanwhile, h+ reacts with H2O to generate •OH (Eq. (7)), and the formed •OH and h+ oxidize methanol to H2O and CO2 (Eq. (8)).

In summary, a novel adsorptive photocatalyst GQDs/Sn3O4 has been successfully synthesized for efficient synergistic removal of U(VI). It achieves a maximum Langmuir adsorption capacity of 226.51 mg·g−1 for U(VI). Compared to pure physicochemical adsorption, this composite material exhibits superior adsorption and visible-light photocatalytic synergism for U(VI) removal within a short photocatalytic equilibrium time. GSO-15 nanocomposite achieves a removal efficiency of 99.67 % after 30 min of visible light irradiation. Mechanism analysis indicates that •O2 − and e− are the primary active species in the photocatalytic process, and the enhancement of photocatalytic activity is attributed to numerous surface active sites, strong visible light absorbability, narrow band gap, and effective separation and transfer of photoinduced e−-h+ pairs. Additionally, the removal efficiency of GSO-15 for U(VI) is minimally affected by coexisting metal ions, and after 5 photoreduction elution cycles, the photocatalytic separation rate for U(VI) remains above 95 %, demonstrating considerable recyclability. This work not only provides novel insights for radioactive environmental remediation but also opens up new opportunities for the application of GQDs/Sn3O4 in photocatalysis.
